perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Confidential Information as it uses for its own confidential, proprietary and trade secret information, but in no case us e less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall endeavor to use a degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Confidential Information as it deems necessary, but in no case should care be less than some degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall use the same degree of care' to 'shall endeavor to use a degree of care as it deems necessary' introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care required. Also changing 'reasonable degree of care' to 'some degree of care' reduces the level of action taken.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing for a lower standard of care than what is required to protect trade secrets under Michigan law. MCL § 445.1903 states that a claim for misappropriation of a trade secret can arise from the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or, at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his/her knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By weakening the requirement to protect the confidential information with less than reasonable care, it may be more difficult to show that the receiving party knew or had reason to know of their duty to maintain its secrecy and they used improper means to misappropriate it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the standard of care required, weakening the protection of confidential information. The original text requires 'the same degree of care' but 'no case less than a reasonable degree of care.' The changed text states the recipient 'endeavor[s] to use a degree of care as it deems necessary, but in no case should care be less than some degree of care.' This potentially allows for a significantly lower standard of care. According to the Law Explanation, MCL § 445.1903 addresses misappropriation of trade secrets, stating that a claim can arise from the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. The change weakens the requirement, it may be difficult to prove that the receiving party used improper means to misappropriate it. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides context on injunctions related to misappropriation, supporting the importance of protecting trade secrets. The change from 'reasonable' to 'some' significantly lowers the bar of protection, creating a higher risk of misappropriation not being actionable, hence a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will not disclose or permit access to Confidential Information to contract workers, consultants or contractors of Recipient or its Affiliatesunless authorized by Disclosing Party in writing and on condition that such persons are bound by obligations ofconfidentiality inuring to the benefit of Disclosing Party and its Affiliates at least as restrictive as th ese terms and conditions.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will try to avoid disclosing or permitting access to Confidential Information to contract workers, consultants or contractors of Recipient or its Affiliates unless authorized by Disclosing Party and if such persons are generally made aware of confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not disclose' to 'will try to avoid disclosing' introduces uncertainty and lowers the bar for compliance. Replacing 'bound by obligations of confidentiality...at least as restrictive' with 'generally made aware of confidentiality' weakens the legal requirement that the contract workers are under the same confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the confidentiality obligations vaguer, it becomes harder to prove a misappropriation claim under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MCL § 445.1903). If the contract workers/consultants/contractors are not subject to obligations of confidentiality, but are merely made generally aware, it would be harder to prove that the receiving party knew or had reason to know of their duty to maintain its secrecy and they used improper means to misappropriate it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated strict confidentiality obligations for contract workers, consultants, and contractors. The changed text weakens this obligation by changing "will not disclose" to "will try to avoid disclosing" and replacing the requirement that such persons be "bound by obligations of confidentiality...at least as restrictive" with the much weaker "generally made aware of confidentiality." As the Law Explanation states, this makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically MCL § 445.1903, discusses misappropriation and injunctions related to trade secrets, which is directly relevant to the confidentiality obligations. The changed text significantly lowers the standard of confidentiality, creating a contradiction because it undermines the ability to enforce trade secret protection, which relies on clear and enforceable confidentiality obligations, especially where contractors are concerned.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall provide written notice to Disclosing Party without undue delay of any misuse or mis appropriation of Confidential Information which may come to the attention of Recipient . The Recipient shall cooperate with and aid the Disclosing Party in mitigating and preventing the unauthorized use and disclosure and any furtherance thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient should try to provide written notice to Disclosing Party when practically possible of any potential misuse of Confidential Information which might possibly come to the attention of Recipient. The Recipient may consider cooperating with the Disclosing Party in mitigating any use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide written notice without undue delay' to 'should try to provide written notice when practically possible' weakens the obligation to report misuse. Changing 'shall cooperate with and aid' to 'may consider cooperating' makes cooperation discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Failure to report potential misuse and cooperate in mitigating damages makes it more difficult to prove damages, which are required to win a claim under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MCL § 445.1903).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the recipient's obligation to report misuse and cooperate, potentially hindering the disclosing party's ability to prove damages under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Specifically, 'shall provide written notice without undue delay' becomes 'should try to provide written notice when practically possible,' and 'shall cooperate with and aid' becomes 'may consider cooperating.' While the scraped snippets do not mention these specific phrases, snippet 1 does state that 'actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined' under MCL § 445.1903. The weakening of the obligation to report and cooperate makes obtaining an injunction more difficult, thus creating a legal contradiction under 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation'.

perturbation.original_text: Disclosing Party may serve written request on Recipient for return or destruction of its Confidential Information at any time up to six (6) months after the termination or expiry of this Agreement and Recipient shall, within thirty (30) days of such request or termination, return to the Disclosing Party (or its designees) or certify as destroyed all Confidential Information, in whatever form, including written or electronically recorded information and al l copies thereof (other than copies retained in automatic back -up and archive systems), provided however that Recipient shall be entitled to retain one copy of the Confidential Information with its legal counsel or other appropriate corporate representativ e to evidence the exchange of information hereunder and in connection with legal or statutory requirements . All such retained copies shall remain subject to the use and disclosure restrictions in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosing Party may request that the Recipient consider returning or destroying its Confidential Information within a reasonable timeframe after the termination or expiry of this Agreement and Recipient will consider such request. Recipient may retain copies of Confidential Information, as deemed appropriate, but will attempt to keep such information confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Disclosing Party may serve written request' to 'Disclosing Party may request that the Recipient consider' makes the request non-binding. Removing the specific timeframe (6 months for request, 30 days for return) introduces uncertainty. Replacing 'shall return...or certify as destroyed' with 'will consider such request' weakens the obligation to return or destroy the information. Replacing 'All such retained copies shall remain subject to the use and disclosure restrictions in this Agreement' with 'but will attempt to keep such information confidential' weakens the confidentiality requirement for retained copies.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information and making the confidentiality of retained copies less strict, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation and makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove misappropriation under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MCL § 445.1903).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the obligations concerning confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information, while the modified text only requires the Recipient to 'consider' such a request. Additionally, the original text enforces strict confidentiality on retained copies, whereas the modified text softens this to merely 'attempt'ing to keep the information confidential. This weakening increases the risk of misappropriation, which MCL § 445.1903(1) addresses by allowing injunctions against actual or threatened misappropriation. By making the return/destruction and confidentiality obligations less stringent, the modified contract undermines the protections intended by trade secret law, specifically making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prevent misappropriation.
